A couple responses:
1. It _does_ own them. That is why there is the cloning functionality of resolvers. The only collections that are mutated are ones that are effectively deep-copied when they are assigned to the stack.
2. This is generally the way resolvers have always worked. The basic idea is that it prevents us from ever needing to resolve the same resolver twice for the same stack. This can be important, since the resolvable attribute might be accessed a _number_ of times over the course of execution.
3. Once we've resolved the resolver, there's no sense in keeping it around; its purpose has been fulfilled.

Hmm I'm going back and forth on this one, since using `"|"` is nice for connoting chained operations... These will be prefixed with `ray::` when displayed, so it might be nice to use a different separator. A few options:
```python
ds = ray.data.range(10, _dataset_name="MyDataset").map().filter().random_shuffle()
```
### Option 1: `|`
```
ray::MyDataset|map|filter|random_shuffle
```
### Option 2: `:`
```
ray::MyDataset:map:filter:random_shuffle
```
### Option 3: `-`
```
ray::MyDataset-map-filter-random_shuffle
```
I find options 1 and 3 to be the most readable. FWIW, Dask uses option 3 for their task keys containing chained operations: https://docs.dask.org/en/stable/10-minutes-to-dask.html#visualize-the-task-graph

From https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html:
> [warnings.warn()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/warnings.html#warnings.warn) in library code if the issue is avoidable and the client application should be modified to eliminate the warning
> [logging.warning()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.warning) if there is nothing the client application can do about the situation, but the event should still be noted

One Thing I Regret from our libmamba experiment is having used `experimental` in the flags and object names. It caused unnecessary disruption and tons of deprecation warnings when we removed it. For context, we're working on adding profiling support for the various ASGI frameworks, and when profiling, we have the concept of an active profile which for the purpose of a web server, it is the thread on which the request is handled.

In this case, we're specifically targeting synchronous request handlers because the way they're handled is by dispatching the handler to another thread. So what we're trying to do here is after it's been dispatched, we want to determine the thread id the handler is running on and update the profile metadata to be aware of that.

The idea is that `conda.anaconda.org` is the default _host_ where these channel names expected to be served:

- `conda-forge` -> `https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge`
- `bioconda` -> `https://conda.anaconda.org/bioconda`

The user can change this default server name to something else (e.g. `my-mirror.org`) and then it would look like:

- `conda-forge` -> `https://my-mirror.org/conda-forge`
- `bioconda` -> `https://my-mirror.org/bioconda`

It's to do with an awkwardness in type-checking vs Optional:

p is Optional[Process], which means p might be None, which means p.start() is not valid.

Previously, `mypy` could figure out that p would never be None because it is directly assigned a Process() value, and so p.start() is safe.

With the abstraction of `ForkProcess`, `mypy` can't narrow the type of `p` down any more.

It *can* figure out that `pp` is safe to call .start() on, apparently, or perhaps just that pp is `Any`.

It's a frustrating clash of this python abstraction style vs static checking.

(an alternative here would be to put a type checking override in... I don't really have any preference)

> Recently I was looking into manually adding co-authors, and apparently you had to leave two empty lines between the message and the trailers, so an `msg = full_commit_msg.rsplit('\n\n', 1)[0]` might work (assuming this is enforced).

The problem with this is that we would need to write a proper detection of trailers or we risk the trailers no longer being trailers. A single split is not enough as we plan on adding a trailer and that means we need to make sure that we add a trailer with other trailers. For example, if this were the original commit message:

```
Commit message

some text
```

Then we can't just simply add it to the last part:

```
Commit message

some text
Co-authored-by: Author Name <author@name.email>
```
On the other hand, we also have to keep all trailers together so if this were the original commit message:

```
Commit message

Co-authored-by: Co-Author Name <co@author.email>
```

Then this would not be acceptable:

```
Commit message

Co-authored-by: Co-Author Name <co@author.email>

Co-authored-by: Author Name <author@name.email>
```

For reference, here's the logic that git uses: https://github.com/git/git/blob/afa70145a25e81faa685dc0b465e52b45d2444bd/trailer.c#L818-L915

@wgtdkp , yes the caller can distinguish this. Note that `At()` returns a  pointer to `Type` (so we have an indirection) and not the `Type` itself.

For example if `Type` is `char *`, then `At()` is `char **At(IndextType)` which returns `char **` (not `char *`).
So for `char **ptr = At(5)`:
- If `(ptr == nullptr)` then caller knows there was no entry at index 5.
- If `(ptr != nullptr)`, then `*ptr` gives the value of `char *` stored in the array and if we have `(*ptr == nullptr)`, then it indicates our `Array<char *>` is holding a `nullptr` at its 5th index.

As written, this code won't necessarily raise a timeout exception, since it could be the case that we wait for completion and never get there, but then the active future is a completed one from the intermediate future.  Imagine that we have `timeout=0.000001` - then we basically skip the `wait()` on the completion event entirely, and we'll just return whatever the result of the first future was, when it finishes (could be success or failure).

Instead, it might be better to check the return value from `wait()` ([docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#threading.Event.wait)) and then if it's False we raise a "timeout" exception ourselves.  That seems better than exposing the intermediate state of whatever random underlying future happens to be active when the timeout expires.

```
detail: a human-readable explanation specific to this occurrence of the problem.

Let's try to think through what the implications are then. Issue is that in [error objects](https://jsonapi.org/format/#error-objects) there is no field required. So what could happen is that someone has a custom error object only with one member which then would be formatted as error. A workaround for such a use case would be simple though for a DJA user - they could simply add a second error object member. What the issue is though that such an error would not be noticed especially critical for existing users. This we should certainly avoid.

What I noticed is that an validation error detail always seems to be a [list](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/exceptions.py#L152) in the [error objects](https://jsonapi.org/format/#error-objects) none of the member may be a list though.

I haven't thoroughly checked but a check could be adjusted the following:
* in case an error is a dict, only has one key and the value of this key is a list, we execute format_error_object
* otherwise we simply append it as before

This would be more defensive and not affecting existing users. Not sure whether I have touched all corner cases but let me know what you think.

Newer versions of Sphinx work just fine with newer versions of docutils and this will cause problems on up to date distros that only have newer versions of both packages available. Really what we want to say is if you are using an older version of Sphinx then we require an older version of docutils, but I don't think there's a good way to do that.

In general, this is a bit of an ugly problem that we've hit before with other dependencies. In those cases, we've done nothing and left it up to the user and/or their package manager/maintainers to ensure compatible versions of our dependencies are installed. This certainly isn't elegant, but it hasn't caused any issues. Distro package maintainers (usually) test packages before pushing them live and we wouldn't be the only Python package that would have issues here. For people installing from `pip`, `pip` prefers the latest version of everything which usually has all the latest bug fixes.

It's probably a good idea to mention in a comment here that `docutils<0.13.1` is required when used with `Sphinx<1.5.1` and provide a link to the GitHub issue, but I think the actual dependency should be on an unpinned back version of docutils.

So I think this depends on the scope of the function, i.e., who needs it and why. 

Forget about PY2 for a minute, let's consider just PY3.

The compressor codecs ``ZLib``, ``BZ2`` and ``LZMA`` require an object that exports the new-style buffer interface (e.g., ``memoryview``) with C-contiguous memory. So for them, it is useful to reshape the input, because it ensures a C-contiguous array, whether the input is C-contiguous, F-contiguous or non-contiguous. IIUC if the input is either C- or F-contiguous then calling ``reshape(-1)`` **will not** result in a copy. If the input is non-contiguous then a copy may occur, but then this is unavoidable - the alternative would be to raise an error that the input needs to be contiguous.

However, other codecs may have less stringent requirements. E.g., they may need a numpy array, but may not care if it is contiguous.

So possibly we should have more than one convenience function for obtaining a numpy array, to account for these differences, with some sort of clear naming scheme, so it is clear which function you should use depending on what requirements you have.

> When A & B will try to set these duplicated ids in the database, there are chances the client code will through errors.

You are correct.  That happens when the isolation level set to `REPEATABLE READ` (or less strict). When prototype was in that mode, occasionally two processes will have the correct timing, and both grab the same block at the same time.

With isolation level set to `SERIALIZABLE`, we get ACID compliance: We can assume only one process can enter the `with transaction.atomic()` section at a time:

* A requires a block of ids
* B requires a block of ids
* A reads the count (`N`) out of the database, and locks the record 
* B tries to reads the same count (`N`) out of the database, but is blocked 
* A increments by 100
* A updates the count in the database (`N+100), and releases lock
* B reads the count (`N+100`) out of the database
* B increments by 100
* B updates the count in the database (`N+200) 

Each process will have a unique block.

I've added a comment explaining that it's an internal thing inside subprocess to the code. A fuller explanation is:
subprocess.Popen() accepts either "a sequence of program arguments or else a single string" as a command to run.
On POSIX, a string means "the exact command, with no arguments" whereas a byte sequence means "the command and arguments" - I guess this is why testinfra encodes the string in general.
On Windows, a string means "the exact, escaped command, including arguments", and a list means "a command with args that needs to be escaped/joined". 

The fun comes on Windows as the encode() causes the following:
string "echo hello" passed to run_local()
string converted to b"echo hello"
b"echo hello" passed to subprocess.Popen
Popen checks the type, finds it's not a string, and runs list2cmdline()
list2cmdline expects a list, but gets a sequence of bytes, eventually blowing up at https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/v3.7.3/Lib/subprocess.py#L530 as iterating over a sequence of bytes leads to integers.

According to the schema, ``starting_frame`` is described as:

```
Each external image may contain one or more consecutive frames of the full ImageSeries. This attribute serves as an index to indicate which frames each file contains, to faciliate random access. The ‘starting_frame’ attribute, hence, contains a list of frame numbers within the full ImageSeries of the first frame of each file listed in the parent ‘external_file’ dataset. Zero-based indexing is used (hence, the first element will always be zero). For example, if the ‘external_file’ dataset has three paths to files and the first file has 5 frames, the second file has 10 frames, and the third file has 20 frames, then this attribute will have values [0, 5, 15]. If there is a single external file that holds all of the frames of the ImageSeries (and so there is a single element in the ‘external_file’ dataset), then this attribute should have value [0].
```

I.e., ``starting_frame`` describes for each ``external_file`` what index the first frame in that file corresponds to in the whole image series. In this way, if we want to access a particular frame,  ``starting_frame`` allows us to identify which ``external_file`` contains that frame and what the index of that frame within the external file is. Without this information one would need to open all external files to figure out how many frames each contains in order to find a particular frame.

This is the actual fix right here. When I changed the ConnectionManager, I accidentally omitted this situation coverage.

Before, the logic was:
* If the profile, region, and role were all None, then...
    * if the stack_name was cached...
        * use the cached profile, region, and role by that cached stack name
     * otherwise, use THIS stack's profile, region, and role
* Otherwise, use the explicitly specified profile, region and role


After v4 was released, the logic was:
* If the stack_name was cached...
    * if the profile, region, and role were all None, then...
        * use the cached profile, region and role
     * otherwise, use the  specified profile, region, and role, using the cached value when no value has been specified
         * _This was a gap in our old logic, so this change was intentional and fixed some things._
* otherwise, use the  specified profile, region, and role, using the current stack's value when no value has been specified

**The gap that I found that was causing some bugs** was when the stack_name was **not** cached, but the profile, region, and role were passed as None. It turns out the !stack_output_external resolver would trigger this exact situation. But this wouldn't actually show up in terms of blow-ups when the user had a profile with a region or a default region specified as an environment variable. 

With this fix, the logic is now:
* If the stack_name was cached...
    * if the profile, region, and role were all None, then...
        * use the cached profile, region and role
     * otherwise, use the  specified profile, region, and role, using the cached value when no value has been specified
         * _This was a gap in our old logic, so this change was intentional and fixed some things._
* otherwise...
    * If the profile, region, and role are all None, then...
        * Use the current_stack's profile, region, and role  
    * Otherwise, use the specified profile, region, and role, using the current stack's value when no value has been specified

Yes, the change is necessary: `collections.abc` is a submodule, and must
be imported to be available on the `collections` object:

```python
>>> import collections
>>> collections.abc.Callable
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.7/collections/__init__.py", line 55, in __getattr__
    raise AttributeError(f'module {__name__!r} has no attribute {name!r}')
AttributeError: module 'collections' has no attribute 'abc'
```

(Note that `os.path` is a special case: the `os` module itself
explicitly [installs an `os.path` module][1] because it’s dynamically
chosen from `posixpath` or `ntpath` at runtime.)

I considered `from collections import abc`, but decided that it was
confusing given that `abc` is also a standard library module that we use
fairly frequently.

[1]: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/58a4054760bffbb20aff90290dd0f3554f7bea42/Lib/os.py#L93

why not have this as part of the graphql config?

checking `os.getenv()` and calling `asbool` every time is going to have a performance hit.

e.g.

```
config._add(
    "graphql",
    dict(
        _default_service="graphql",
        resolvers_enabled=asbool(os.getenv("DD_TRACE_GRAPHQL_RESOLVERS_ENABLED", default=False)),
    ),
)
```

Then we can just check: `if config.graphql.resolvers_enabled`. This is more consistent with the way other integrations are setup, and allows someone to change this via programmatic config instead of env variables only.

Unmanaged metadata is, by definition, not part of the managed package, but it can still be an important part of delivering a full product to customers. The end goal is not to install a package; the end goal is to deliver a fully configured product. Sometimes doing that requires deploying unmanaged metadata before or after the managed package is installed.

> Is there a better way to convey how integral force-app folder/this metadata is to a managed package?

The default package directory (usually called force-app) defines what will be included when you release a managed package from your cci project (or when you release an unlocked package, if you're doing that, or if you are not releasing the package at all but running the `deploy` task to get the metadata into an org in unmanaged form). It's the right place to put metadata except for when there's a reason to put it somewhere else (like one of the reasons I listed above).

I'm not sure I see the benefit of providing the identifying info for the blob to write on only the first request in the stream.  It's a very small amount of overhead relative to the blob size itself, and I think the client side logic is simpler if it has to maintain less state about which chunk it's on.  In general it seems cleaner if the first chunk sent doesn't look any different from an arbitrary chunk _n_ (except for the final chunk).

The server side already has to keep track of the state, so there's no change there (except it should validate that it's the same blob being written and fail the request if not, but this is nice since it's a cheap check and helps catch client errors and avoids ambiguity).

Making the "key" to WriteBlobRequest mandatory is also just nice structurally since then even in the future if we pass around those request objects (or conceivably want to do something fancy like multiplex several blob writes into a single streaming RPC), we never have to worry about losing track of which blob we're talking about.

> What would an input state difference be? I'm not sure I follow how we could have a state difference and not also have an input difference?

Consider a resource `R` with `(inputs, state) => ({foo: bar}, {foo: bar})`. If we refresh `R` and the provider returns `({foo: bar}, {foo: baz})`, the provider is still allowed to indicate that `R` needs to be updated on a subsequent run even if the program presents the inputs `{foo: bar}` b/c `foo` has different properties in the inputs and the state. This is arguably a bug, and I'm not sure it happens in practice, but it is a "feature" of the model that we might need to consider.

More common is the case where an input has no corresponding entry in the state, so diffs are only detectable from input to input (e.g. k8s secrets).

> the later would allow someone to come and change the dict to something like {b: 2, d: 4} and the plan would allow the value {c: 3, d: 4} but not allow the value {a: 1, c: 3}.

I think the latter would still allow `{a: 1, c: 3}` b/c the `same` on `c` is constrained to the `add` in the diff. I think the argument here is the same as for the analogous case at top level in that we want to allow the base state to differ as long as the diffs are compatible.

The hash is mainly used to ensure the correct lines are marked as covered, to my knowledge. Upstream's `genhtml`, for example, which uses an lcov file and the original source tree to produce an html page with annotated source files, will throw an error if it finds a hash that mismatches: https://github.com/linux-test-project/lcov/blob/master/bin/genhtml#L5308.

Genhtml is of course an unlikely usecase with pycoverage, but it's the reference consumer.

It is up to the implementation of the consumer to define what the md5 is used for, but it is marked as optional, so any tools that break on a missing md5 are inherently broken. There shouldn't be any issue with simply omitting the hash when it can't be set, and I don't think I can think of many other uses than "has our user accidentally changed their source". I think that was probably a more common use case around the time lcov added that feature :)

It *would* be sad if pycoverage produced different outputs depending on the machine it is run on. Maybe hence it's best to remove this functionality in general.

I think a fifth option is to lobby upstream for supporting a more modern hash, as much as that seems unnecessary. It's honestly a bit silly that python mandates thinking about FIPS compliance with no opt-out if you use its md5 hash, and even more silly that some default python distributions seem to enable FIPS compliance (this seems to be a MacOS thing?).

It'll be hard to come up with a backwards-compatible design for that, so I suspect the solution would be to deprecate having hashes at all, since those don't really seem that useful anymore in 2022 when checking for accidental source changes is anyway so easy and often unnecessary.

> What about if plugin starts with a dot, it means an extension, and thus a format hint?

Yes. I separated them, because plugins and formats are different things. This is ultimately how we ended up with hybrid format names like `PNG-FI`, `PNG-PIL`, but also with format names (that are actually plugins) `FFMPEG` or `SPE`. Separating them into `plugin=` (only plugin names allowed here) and `format=` (only formats allowed here) makes this cleaner. 

> This is how it worked previously.

Indeed, this is something we introduced to support the v2 API through imopen. It won't work in the v3 API, where the value of `plugins` is checked against ... well plugins (`plugin in config.known_plugins`). If we do end up merging this, I will refactor the internals of the v2 API to separate plugins and formats and send them to the appropriate arguments. This will keep the behavior local to the v2 API and allow us to have a cleaner backend.

What's more is that calling `PNG-PIL` doesn't actually guarantee that the data is treated as a PNG. The v3 pillow plugin currently has no notion of format (it hands a file-like object to pillow and, if that fails, concludes that pillow can't read it). The v2 plugin does have a notion of format (enforced via file extension), but if that fails (e.g. for bytes that have no extension) it also just does the same thing as the v3 plugin: call the backend and see if things fail. Notably, neither plugin restricts the formats pillow may use internally (this could be done though), so at the moment iio.imread(some-jpg-bytes, format="PNG-PIL") will look like it loads a PNG from a JPEG, but it actually just uses pillows JPEG decoder and ignores the `format=` part silently. Because of this, the format is a _hint_ and not enforced. 

I felt like this should be documented as such, in an effort to be more transparent and, while at it, I figured it would be useful to have a similar functionality in v3.

Brownie points: we no longer have to figure out which format supports which extension and create strange virtual formats like `PNG-PIL` and alike. `plugins` is everything listed in `config.known_plugins` and `format_hint` is everything listed in `config.known_extensions` and the rest is mix and match. If the format being hinted is actually not what is in the file, that's totally fine; it's a hint anyway, and all it does is make the selection process slower, because we try unsuitable plugins first due to the wrong hint.

BTW, My favorite feature is the release-drafter is that you can add the label or fix the PR title after the merge and at the next run, it will correct the draft. Still once a release is made, the bot will not touch the text, only humans can edit it.

Of course each entries have their unique address, which means, they are presenting different data consuming for this node's output. But, if some entries are the same(same node, index and version), then they should still be the 'same' after subgraph partition(same means the entry value is the same among those entries, but of course the entry value is different from before partition).
Take your example as example,
After partition, the result would be
```
nodes : [ 
  { name : "SubgraphA" }, #subgraph_nodes[0], have 2 outputs, the output[1] presents the origin output of  NodeA
  { name : "NodeB",   #nodes[1]
    inputs : [[0,1,0]] }, #pointing at SubgraphA, repsents the output data require of NodeA 
  { name : "NodeC",  #nodes[2]
    inputs : [[0,1,0]] } #pointing at SubgraphA, repsents the output data require of NodeA 
]
```
So, here's what we can do to simplify the work in `ConnectSubgraphOutputs` 

1. Create a map about unique_entry -> origin entry address(Not necessary, just for better performance).
2. Pass unique_entry to `ConnectSubgraphOutputs`, and return a map of  unique_entry -> unique_entry_after_partition.
3. Then we know, how each original entry should be replaced for. Then do the replacement on all entry address based on above 2 maps.

What I what to do here is, we should avoid backend developer to handle the duplicated entries, instead, backend should only tells the new connection after partition. Take above case as example, backend developer should only tells that the output data requirement on NodeA now should pointing to index 1 on new subgraph node through `ConnectSubgraphOutputs`. And build_subgraph pass should propagate this change on all original entries which points at NodeA.

I think connexion started out as a tool focused on json apis, and so there is some very old and very weird coupling with json.
In my opinion, the number one improvement I'd like to see from connexion is to decouple content types (see my work here https://github.com/zalando/connexion/pull/760 ), but it would mean a backwards incompatible change, and so a major version bump.

With respect to this particular section, I agree - it doesn't think it makes any sense to jsonify everything.
My guess though, is that because connexion is so forgiving about not specifying response content type, there are a bunch of users who are not specifying a json content type for their response, and are relying on this (and other) bugs/assumptions to get json out the other end.

I think the only thing to do in the context of this PR is leave it for now, and then if we can muster the effort - work together to decouple content type properly and do a major version bump.

I'm very interested to pick #760 back up, and to collaborate on this, assuming @hjacobs will have enough bandwidth to review the changes and merge them.

It happened so that I re-wrote the whole module yesterday and spent some time figuring out exact directions and quantities of each transform. I concluded that the rotation should be CCW, which led me to finding this bug. 

The reasoning is as follows:
1. The Y axis in images points down, hence the positive rotation should be CW. Consequently, the negative direction is CCW.
2. Affine transform used here is the inverse (aka backward, pull, etc.)  transform (see [affine_transform](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.ndimage.affine_transform.html)). Therefore, it "negates" its arguments. Hence, for positive angle, the rotation is negative, namely, CCW. 
Similarly, for positive translation, the image moves in the negative direction. 

P. S.
As an additional sanity check,  I verified that the behavior here matches [scikit-image](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.AffineTransform)

What do people think about changing this if statement to 
`if http_response.raw is None:`?

The handler function is only needed to manually parse a raw response into a parsed response since it can't be modeled correctly. The precondition for this function is that this a real response we got from the service. And it has a raw_response, and an incorrect parsed_response that needs to be manually built  from the raw response. It should skip if this was a stubbed response as there is no raw response, only a preset parsed response.

I think it makes more sense to check this as directly as possible. `parse_get_bucket_location` depends on the raw response, so we should just check it. If it isnt there, there is nothing to do. The current if statements have the same effect of skipping the function in the correct cases, but they don't really do a good job of explaining why. They check for the side effects of the case where there is no raw response. We could then update the comment to say that there is no raw response to parse, this is likely a stubbed response.

@patrickBerry, `()` is already immutable, so that half of this PR is invalid.

The `kwargs={}` half might actually be valid, in general, since that dict instance is essentially a module global that anyone who can get a reference to it can mutate for everyone else.
```python
>>> class Foo:
...     def __init__(self, kwargs={}):
...         self.kwargs = kwargs
... 
>>> f1 = Foo()
>>> f2 = Foo()
>>> f2.kwargs['global'] = True
>>> f1.kwargs
{'global': True}
```
But rather than changing this to `kwargs=None`, it seems cleaner to use `**kwargs`. Then each call that doesn't supply any kwargs results in `kwargs` getting bound to a new empty dict, resolving this concern, and the code on line 73 doesn't actually have to change.

ARM actually supports CAE on the service side and SDK side (by handling CAE challenge claims), so defaulting `enable_cae` to true maintains this behavior of CAE tokens being requested for these credentials.

It would look like (I already tested it)

```python 
def set_investment_period(n, periods):
    """
    Set the investment periods of the network.

    If the network snapshots are a pandas.MultiIndex, the investment periods
    have to be a subset of the first level. If snapshots are a single index,
    they and all time-series are repeated for all periods. This changes
    the network snapshots to be a MultiIndex (inplace operation) with the first
    level being the investment periods and the second level the snapshots.

    Parameters
    ----------
    n : pypsa.Network
    periods : list
        List of periods to be selected/initialized.

    Returns
    -------
    None.

    """
    if isinstance(n.snapshots, pd.MultiIndex):
        assert set(periods).issubset(n.snapshots.unique('period')), (
            "Not all investment periods are in level `period` of snapshots.")
        n._investment_periods = periods
    else:
        # Convenience case:
        # repeat the timeseries for each period > all multiindexed
        for component in n.all_components:
            pnl = n.pnl(component)
            attrs = n.components[component]["attrs"]

            for k,default in attrs.default[attrs.varying].iteritems():
                pnl[k] = pd.concat({p: pnl[k] for p in periods})

        n._snapshots = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([periods, n.snapshots],
                                                  names=['period', 'snaphots'])
        n._snapshot_weightings = pd.concat({p: n.snapshot_weightings for p in periods})
```

Then we separate the cases: 
1. `set_snapshots` -> only hard reindexing 
2. `set_investment_periods` -> if network is single-indexed convert all to multi-indexes with repeated time-series/weightings

A few things are needed to make this bulletproof:
1. We don't need to care about CPython, stackless, pypy, etc here, so best not to track
2. What about "==3.4.5",">3.4,<3.5" etc?

Given this, there are only 2 robust options I can see.
1. Drop `pkg_resources.{Distribution,Requirement}`, and leverage `packaging.{requirements,specifiers,version}` to parse these things fully and then get down to the (operator, version) level to implement a robust test of whether a constraint includes some version of python 3.
2. Whitelist constraints instead.

Option 2 should be much simpler and - I think - get you what you need. The idea would be to whitelist the value of the `python-setup.interpreter_constraints` option taking that as the default for the repo and which currently hardcode defaults to `['>=2.7,<3']`. Python does not allow for mutually recursive modules. In order to break the dependency, we have to make a forward reference to the 'Resource' type. The runtime semantics of type ascription are that the interpreter literally evals the type and checks that it doesn't throw. Since `Resource` evals to the Resource class, it would fail because of the initialization cycle.

The official workaround is to refer to the type in quotes, which has clear runtime semantics (eval of a string is a string) and the type checker understands.

I think it is nearly strictly better to just do `.format(new_definition)`. If the `__str__` method is overridden then stringifying the element is likely to produce useful information. If it is not overridden, then `__str__` defaults to `__repr__` which almost definitely produces at least the same amount of information as `type(new_definition).__name__`, (the default implementation includes the type name). Furthermore, `__repr__` method is often overridden by directive defintions so it'd be a good idea to use it.

While this iteration works for this particular use case, it's inaccurate. The array you are iterating over contains pairs of (4-byte) integers; the first one is the frequency you're looking for (little-endian). For example, here's the array you're parsing from an [online M1 dump](https://github.com/xzhih/one-key-hidpi/files/5612845/ioreg_output_m1mac.txt), split into the pairs of numbers: ``` 0046c323 0d030000 00475a31 0d030000 0048f13e 0d030000 0049884c 20030000 002f6859 2c030000 0030ff66 3f030000 00319674 61030000 00322d82 8d030000 00fd558e b9030000 00c87e9a eb030000 005d39a5 1d040000 004319b2 39040000 003689b8 39040000 00a265bb 39040000 0029f9be 39040000 ``` The min value you want is 0x23c34600, the first integer in the array. The max value you want is 0xbef92900, the second-to-last integer in the array. Here's a screenshot of my M2 Max E-core values: My min E-core freq is 0x365c0400 (912 MHz). My max E-core freq is 0x907b4e00 (2.424 GHz). And the P-core values: My min P-core freq is 0x29d7ab80 (702 MHz). My max P-core freq is 0xdc4c7c00 (3.696 GHz). So you could (probably) grab from index 0 and index (length - 8) and avoid iteration. However, I'm only guessing that the array is always sorted in increasing order, as I haven't found that documented anywhere but have confirmed it with every sample output I could get my hands on. If you do iterate, I would increment by 8 rather than 4. That will save you the need to do your `curr_freq > 1e6` test below.

Guess my main point is that:
`projects.yml` just lists all projects in use / required for the `self`-project to work.

Then user can use:
- `west` to fetch and parse `projects.yml` to stay up-to-date with latest Zephyr
- Read `projects.yml`manually, and use those to fork / copy /  whatever into his own system, to base his own development on Zephyr. But in this example, he may have no intention of keeping up-to-date with Zephyr development. Kind of what was asked by @aescolar on: How to use Zephyr without `west`
- make his own script / tool to fetch sub-projects

So `projects.yml` has no expectations on how / which tool you use, it just describes projects.
Anything having `west` in its name, starts to imply that `west` is required.

I thought about this a bit too. We mostly upload a known set of file extensions, and falling back to `application/binary` isn't the worst. But there are no guarantees we won't upload a new file extension, or a dozen, in the future. So we could:

- have a small subset of known file extensions and potentially adjust this map every time we add a new extension
- have a full set of known file extensions, and potentially adjust this map more rarely, if we upload a brand new file extension that doesn't exist in the full set
- have a small subset of file extensions to override the system mimetypes, but fall back to the system definitions. This is what we have here.

In each of these cases, we could require a scriptworker release, or we could configure the known file extensions via the `scriptworker.yaml` config file per scriptworker instance.

In practice I'm not sure the win is large enough to warrant further change, but I could be wrong.

I belive your argument is wrong and let's take line 117<Insert line here> as an example. We have the
channel ``Node(0)`` <-> ``Node(4)``, but the channel does not have the required capacity for the 
payment to be performed so it choses the other route which is through 
``Node(0)`` <-> ``Node(1)`` <-> ``Node(2)`` <-> ``Node(4)`` (this was also mentioned down).



The capacity of each channel on the network will look like this afterwords:

|   Node  | Partner | Capacity |
|:-------:|---------|----------|
| Node(0) | Node(1) |    50    |
| Node(0) | Node(4) |    10    |
| Node(1) | Node(0) |    150   |
| Node(1) | Node(2) |    50    |
| Node(2) | Node(1) |    150   |
| Node(2) | Node(4) |    100   |
| Node(2) | Node(3) |    101   |
| Node(3) | Node(2) |    101   |
| Node(4) | Node(0) |    25    |
| Node(4) | Node(2) |    50    |


As you mentioned then the payment 

``- transfer: {from: 2, to: 1, amount: 150, expected_http_status: 200}`` is performed. And the 
channel capacity will look like this.


|   Node  | Partner | Capacity |
|:-------:|---------|----------|
| Node(0) | Node(1) |    50    |
| Node(0) | Node(4) |    10    |
| Node(1) | Node(0) |    150   |
| Node(1) | Node(2) |    200   |
| Node(2) | Node(1) |    0     |
| Node(2) | Node(4) |    100   |
| Node(2) | Node(3) |    101   |
| Node(3) | Node(2) |    101   |
| Node(4) | Node(0) |    25    |
| Node(4) | Node(2) |    50    |


Which leaves the channel ``Node(2)`` <-> ``Node(1)`` with 0 capacity, as you mentioned. 

Next is the ``- transfer: {from: 2, to: 1, amount: 150, expected_http_status: 409}``
which works as expected because there is no capacity neither in the ``Node(2) <-> ``Node(1)`` channel
nor in the ``Node(2) <-> Node(4) <-> Node(0) <-> Node(1)``. 

But the transfer ``- transfer: {from: 2, to: 1, amount: 1}`` should work, beacuse event though 
``Node(2) <-> Node(1)`` route doesn't have the required capacity, the other route availabel has the required capacity
``Node(2) <-> Node(4) <-> Node(0) <-> Node(1)``.


This is the same as the transfer ``from 0 to 4`` where there was no capacity in the 
``Node(0)`` <-> ``Node(4)`` but enough on the other route: 
``Node(0) <-> Node(1) <-> Node(2) <-> Node(4)`` because it has the capacity.

@KellenSunderland If data loader is configured with rgb_mean=(0,0,0), then the in_data for first convolution are all non-negative values, we can directly quantize the input to uint8 and don't need to ignore the first convolution. The flow will look like
input ->quantize->convolution(uint8)
If data loader is configured with rgb_mean!=(0,0,0), then in_data for first convolution will have negative values. For this case, you need to exclude the first convolution to run it in fp32 mode, and after the relu following first convolution, do quantization to uint8. The flow will look like,
input ->convolution(fp32) -> relu(fp32) ->quantize->pooling(uint8)

Let me try add more details (from what I recall) on our internal project and how it worked:

- It was all running on an RTOS platform. All modules were part of the firmware running on NPC (OT stack, radio arbiter and driver, and other (legacy) stacks) all part of NCP firmware. 
- OT stack had its own RTOS task:
   - OT stack requires that all the OT public and platform APIs and callbacks to be invoked from same OS context, e.g. same RTOS task.
- Arbiter and radio driver had their own shared RTOS task (and I think other/legacy stack ran on its own RTOS task as well).
- I think the different tasks synced using ROTS locks/semaphore.

Arbiter module provided APIs to all entities which wanted to control the radio:
- For example, `otPlatRadio` APIs to OT stack and very similar set of APIs to the other (legacy) stack.
- Arbiter module managed state variables per entity to remember the last request/command (API call). 
   - For example, if `otPlatRadioSleep()` was called to put radio to sleep, or `otPlatRadioReceive()` to rx, or a request to tx frame or perform energy scan, etc.
- It then handled the requests/commands from different entities and decided what to do actually with the radio. Here are some of the rules I recall:
   - We put radio to sleep mode only when all entities requested it to sleep.
   - When different entities requested radio to be in rx mode: 
        - If the channels matched, it was straight-forward and we put the radio in rx mode on that channel.
        - But if the requests were for different channels, I think we had a simple rule to prioritize one entity (I think OT stack request won on the rx channel).
        - Just a note on this: 
            - In our use-case, all stacks (OT and legacy) were actually operating on the same channel, but we still could have the situation that they put radio on different channels, for example to support a discover/mac scan operation (during which we can switch to different channels).
            - We also had the idea to use a windowed rx model when we have conflicting rx channels (rx on first channel for some time interval, then switch to other channel, an so on) but I think we did not implement/use this model.
   - If we get a tx request by an entity while radio was busy with an ongoing tx from another entity we just waited for the current tx to complete (success or failure) and then proceeded with the next tx request.

I wonder if we should consider using the [flex-direction](https://css-tricks.com/snippets/css/a-guide-to-flexbox/#aa-flex-direction) property rather than a `float` here.

Our use of `flex` is complicated by our continued support for IE11 but there are only a handful of [pretty specific bugs with flex in IE11](https://caniuse.com/flexbox) -- but `flex-direction` doesn't seem to be one that would cause issues.

One thing we'd need to test and be sure of, however, is that the LTR/RTL changes are reflected properly if we use `flex-direction`. Part of the motivation here is avoiding the use of `float` where, specifically, the wrapping of text isn't involved. It's not the end of the world to use it but something worth considering I think. Here is a short [blog I found interesting about float](https://css-tricks.com/is-css-float-deprecated/).

Overall I'd suggest trying it out, testing in both LTR and RTL and if it causes more problems than it's worth then the `float` should be okay.

We can use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` for more test cases. For example:
```python

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    (
        'attestation_1_slot, attestation_1_justified_slot, attestation_2_slot, attestation_2_justified_slot, expected'
    ),
    [
        (0, 0, 0, 0, False),
        (4, 3, 3, 2, False),  # not (attestation_data_1.justified_slot < attestation_data_2.justified_slot
        (4, 0, 3, 1, False),  # not (attestation_data_2.justified_slot + 1 == attestation_data_2.slot)
        (4, 0, 4, 3, False),  # not (attestation_data_2.slot < attestation_data_1.slot)
        (4, 0, 3, 2, True),

    ],
)
def test_is_surround_vote(sample_attestation_data_params,
                          attestation_1_slot,
                          attestation_1_justified_slot,
                          attestation_2_slot,
                          attestation_2_justified_slot,
                          expected):
    attestation_data_1_params = {
        **sample_attestation_data_params,
        'slot': attestation_1_slot,
        'justified_slot': attestation_1_justified_slot,
     }
    attestation_data_1 = AttestationData(**attestation_data_1_params)
    attestation_data_2_params = {
        **sample_attestation_data_params,
        'slot': attestation_2_slot,
        'justified_slot': attestation_2_justified_slot,
    }
    attestation_data_2 = AttestationData(**attestation_data_2_params)
    assert is_surround_vote(attestation_data_1, attestation_data_2) == expected

In the current interface design, the implementation is responsible for identifying what `@p aFullName` is. It can be either of:
1. service name
2. service instance name
3. host name

I think it's possible to correctly identify the `instance name` even when it contains a `.` in it. So, the interface should have included enough information. 

The benefit of the current interface is that the DNS-SD server in OT core don't need to further split the full name into `instance name` and `full service name`, which needs 256B buffers. 

The alternative is to provide different interfaces for different names:
1. otDnssdQuerySubscribeService(aFullServiceName)
2. otDnssdQuerySubscribeServiceInstance(aFullServiceName, aInstanceName)
3. otDnssdQuerySubscribeHost(aFullHostName)

Together with the 3 corresponding "Unsubscribe" APIs, there will be totally 6 callbacks to setup. I prefer the current interfaces (with only 2 callbacks) because it's simpler for the OT core.

Invenio-Files-REST provides a Celery task (`invenio_files_rest.tasks.migrate_file`) to migrate a existing files from the current location to a new location. Given a `file_id` and the `name` of the new location (which should have been already created), it will:
1. create a new empty FileInstance in the destination location
2. copy the file content in the newly created FileInstance
3. re-link all ObjectVersions pointing to the previous FileInstance to the new one
4. optionally, re-compute the file checksum

This task can be used to migrate all files or a subset of files in case of location change.

Hey update on this - `eval_at` actually does not have ay aliases, at least not as a fit kwarg. These aliases are supported as constructor `params`, but `eval_at` is the only way to specify ranking threshold depth at `fit` time.

I had the impression that "operational lock" is meant to synchronize "operations" on the leaser that can be initiated from external threads (e.g. `start` and `stop`), and used it to make the `add` / `remove` operations atomic. If this causes confusion, it might indeed make sense to use a separate lock, e.g. named `add_remove_lock`?

The motivation behind this is the observation in debug logs that `add()` and `remove()` operations can be intertwined if invoked from different threads at just the "right" moment. Both operations modify the internal `_leased_messages` dict and the `_bytes` counter.

The dict is probably fine, because `ack_id` is unique for every message, but I wouldn't say the same for `_bytes`.

 1. `_marshal_model`  will be renamed to `_marshal_object` to keep consistency with the unmarshaling flow

Yes, but here explain how to create a new branch, then how to add the changes (git add) and commit them, and finally says how to push and do a PR. 
So, I propose you change two paragraphs:

**This**
In Neurosim-lab/netpyne repo, you must not work in the `master` branch, there is a branch called `development` for contribuiting. Therefore, if you want to add a new change, you should first synchronize your local `development` branch with the `upstream` repository, then make your changes in `development` branch. Also you can create a new branch based on `development` and [check it out](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-checkout) so that any changes you make will exist on that new branch:

```bash
 ~$ git checkout development            # switch to local development branch
 ~$ git fetch upstream                  # get the current state of the remote upstream repo
 ~$ git merge upstream/development      # synchronize local development branch with remote upstream development branch
 ~$ git checkout -b new-branch          # create local branch "new-branch" and check it out
```

> **Alternative**
> You can save some typing by using `~$ git pull upstream/development` to replace the `fetch` and `merge` lines above.

> **Note:** You could synchronize your local `master` branch with that of the `upstream` repository to have the updated version, but you **_must never_** make any changes on the `master` branch.

**And this**
Now, it’s time to organize your changes into a series of [commits](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit). Commits are like snapshots of the repository — actually, more like a description of what has to change to get from the most recent snapshot to the current snapshot.

This is a bit magical, as it's the only case where the value type of a list-typed option is not "list of member_type" but "flattened list of something else". 

we can make this less magical by having a `flattened_list` option type, which behaves like a list in all respects except that its value is the flattening of all member values, if they are lists themselves. 

Then we have two orthogonal pieces of behavior - shlexing a single string into a list, and flattening a list of lists into a single flat list - that together give us the effect we want, without any magic, and without any interaction between the option type and the member type.

AFAICT all `get_docs` calls have an encoder supplied which should in turn get here via https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/105fe2055562adbeb7c5ae41f97712794bf8716b/src/cloudant/_common_util.py#L252

However, I noticed that the view call to `get_docs` was slightly different in using the design document's encoder rather than the client one. 
https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/105fe2055562adbeb7c5ae41f97712794bf8716b/src/cloudant/view.py#L238

I think this is correct because if the design document has an encoder set it will be used in preference to the client one, but otherwise fall back to the client encoder if unset (via https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/105fe2055562adbeb7c5ae41f97712794bf8716b/src/cloudant/document.py#L68).

I think the caveat here is that someone who set an encoder specifically on a document (and no client level encoder) would also want to set that encoder on their design document before making view requests to guarantee the same encoder was used for the fields in the documents and the keys in the view query.

I don't think there is a problem compared to current behaviour but I wanted to call attention to it in case anyone had other thoughts.

* Logically `WorkflowStepFunction` is wrapped around the original function, it represents the original function instead of the workflow. Workflow is only an interface to trigger execute, saving inputs and DAG dependencies.
* Physically we should export the remote function upon `workflow.step`, so other worker in the Ray cluster can access this remote function earlier; more importantly, it prevents exporting the same function again and again if we generate the workflow multiple times from the same workflow step function.

We could have a period of experimenting with DataClasses and seeing whether there are any downsides.

We might discover that there are some cases where we might still like NamedTuples for minor reasons:

1. Immutability can make reasoning slightly easier.
2. We often import "T" and therefore T.NamedTuple can be used with one less import
3. In the Snowfakery context (and perhaps other bulkdata contexts) NamedTuples would probably be less memory and perhaps other perf benefits? 

I mean if you want mutability or the tons of other features of Dataclasses then of course they are better. But if you "would" have used a tuple before either of them existed (simple pairs and triples) and you just want to use names instead of positions, I don't see a harm with using NamedTuples. The forward migration path from NamedTuples to Dataclasses is probably pretty trivial in almost every case, so if your model evolves towards more complexity it isn't hard to switch. But I'm certainly not a NamedTuple zealot and I might find that there's some detail of dataclasses that is so much better that makes them better by default.

Dick is looking into it, but I don't think sentence `event if we don't fix seed, each dropout result will be the same` is true. What is true though, is the fact that the dropout as is implemented now would not respond to the change of seed on the MXNet side after the initial creation of the op. Seeding each forward would destroy performance, so how about a solution like this - if you know that you will cache the value of seed (like in the dropout descriptor resource case), every time when you get the descriptor it should internally ask the random resource "did a user set a new seed?" (which could be implemented as a set in the random resource that would keep track of who asked about this already and resetting that set when user calls to `mxnet.random.seed`). If the answer is "no", then no reseeding is required, but if the answer is yes, the dropout descriptor should be reseeded with a new value.

>Is this because the normal RPC to add the object location is async?

Yes, the motivation is a combination of (1) reporting the pinned location as early as possible for the sake of future scheduling decisions that might otherwise miss out on that information, and (2) simplifying the `ReferenceCounter::GetLocalityData` logic by ensuring that `ref.pinned_at_raylet_id` is always in `ref.locations`. Before this change, I had a lot of logic around only using `ref.locations` if the object was owned by us, OBOD is turned on, _and_ `!ref.locations.empty()`, otherwise use `ref.pinned_at_raylet_id`, but only if it has a value, etc. Adding the invariant that `ref.locations` always contains the full object locations, as up-to-date as possible, even if the OBOD is turned off (in which case it will only contain the `ref.pinned_at_raylet_id` location), greatly simplified the logic there and elsewhere. A side-benefit is that the OBOD will now have even more up-to-date information.

>I think it makes sense to add it preemptively, but I'm a bit worried about the potential of a memory leak here. Are we certain that this entry will get deleted? Considering message reordering, failures, etc. Actually is message reordering not an issue for the normal Add/Remove RPCs?

We currently make some very strong assumptions about message ordering and network reliability: if the add/remove messages for a given location are reordered or if the remove message never reaches the owner, the location will be added and never removed until the reference is deleted. It was my impression, back when we decided that a failed OBOD --> core worker request is equivalent to the object being freed, that we were punting on these kind of concerns for now. Btw, if anything, adding the `pinned_at_raylet_id` location earlier lessens the chance that message reordering would result in a leak!

Also, do we currently handle message reordering for the GCS-based object directory? I thought that we don't.

>Another option is to open this part in a separate PR, since it doesn't look like it's needed for the main change of using the cached locations.

I was going to, but it does make the `ReferenceCounter::GetLocalityData` logic much, much cleaner, so I somewhat consider it in scope? 😁  If I pulled it out into a different PR, I'd have to reimplement that core `GetLocalityData` logic twice instead of once, in this PR.

All of the user's GUI sits on a different branch in the process tree, the environment of which ROS has no control over. So even if the environment manipulation itself *was* working perfectly (which is also close to impossible because the semantics of environment variable *values* were never meant for the kind of dynamicity you're demanding), you still wouldn't be able to enforce any kind of environment consistency across a user's session. A few thoughts about this:
1. Discriminating between C++ and Python is important to understand how the code is used by internal FB projects.
2. Tracking both C++ and Python will allow us account for situations such as when a team uses directly the C++ backend OR when the Python API along with avpy is used.
3. Using a pseudo-submodule such as `torchvision.csrc.` for all C++ log messages aligns with the structure of our code. 
4. I would favour a simple logging approach over a complex strategy. Hence I would avoid introducing extra arguments just to avoid duplicate logging calls because this complicates the API. 
5. I would also favour the adoption of a simple policy (such as put the log call in all constructors and all public methods), even if this leads to 2 separate calls. Having a simple policy means easier enforcement on PR reviews.

This tri-state is needed to support build-time resolves for foreign platforms. We do not have information to build an environment markers dictionary without a foreign platform's target interpreter present. As such, we can only answer "Maybe" (None) to questions about environment marker applicability. We use this tristate to include distributions without checking environment markers when we can't when building a PEX (i.e.: we err on being overly inclusive). At runtime on the foreign platform, the resolve will be exact an winnow out any extra distributions we shipped over-eagerly. So we get a correct runtime resolve at the cost of potentially including more foreign platform distributions than needed. We can do no better here and this is a fairly narrow edge case.

I don't like having a stack of wrappers not because I think it violates DRY (anyone who saw my tests knows I have no problems hitting cmd+v hitting cmd+v) but mostly because it _feels wrong_. And I think this time there is an actual reason to this.

>it would be akin to saying "whatever extra stuff the put method does"... which would be pretty confusing and implicit to users, since they then need to know that we just-so-happen to be using Session.put later on in the method, which is an implementation detail our users should not have to care about!

I think we already have a much more confusing behavior. If you need to browse the source code to understand what's hiding behind the `kwargs`, if you have to know that the package uses `aiohttp` under the hood to effectively work with it – I agree this is a bit inconvenient, but ultimately this is a virtue because in expands user's true knowledge about the system.
What we did is that we restricted api flexibility by being overly zealous about explicit args. `aiohttp` has a ton of parameters one can customize per-request, but we decided we know better. There are actually 3 timeout parameters, but we only allow one of them.  What I find the most confusing is `session` parameter. Our type hints clearly suggests that we expect an optional `aiohttp.ClientSession` session. A user can put a lot of per-session parameters in there and most of them will work... but not `timeout`, because we force override it on per-request level. And this is truly confusing because a user will rightly assume that session-level timeout will work, but it won't because of an implementation detail.

tl;dr I think saying "this is our method and by the way we will happily pass any kwargs to `aiohttp`, so please consult its docs" is better then "this is out method's signature and that's all you need to know"

The impetus to put schema first came from the fact that you cannot use config in assets without schema (this is a consequence of there already being an implicit schema with the `assets` key for assets). If we want to present assets and ops together (which IMO we should for simplicity's sake), the old ordering becomes very awkward since the first section (accessing config inside compute fn _without_ schema) does not work for assets.

FWIW however, I also find the new ordering more clear generally, especially if we are discouraging config without schema. IMO 99% of the time, people are going to think: "Configuration? Cool, first, where do I define the accepted parameters? Second, how do I access them?" Not: "First, how do I access the parameters? Second, how do I specify them?" It's like you'd typically write a function signature before writing the body.

>we were concerned that asking readers to immediately understand the notion of config schema would make the system less approachable.

I agree that attempting a full presentation of config schema would be undesirable, but the common case (which is used in the example) is simple and virtually self-explanatory: key-value pairs with keys parameter names and values simple types. I mentioned that I have some other improvements for the page in mind, one of these is a greater-depth explanation of schema further down.

>I think the notion that ops, software-defined assets, and resources can be configured should be introduced before introducing schema.

Is this not already introduced beforehand the Overview section?

1. Just use a function to create unit vectors from spherical coordinates. I think we already have them in the doa sub-package, so we could just pull them somewhere more obvious.
2. Make `DirectionVector` a [sub-class of ndarray](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/basics.subclassing.html). This is quite neat. We can make sure the vector is normalized in the constructor, and then, we can still use this class as input to any numpy method. We can also add methods to convert back to azimuth/colatitude. I went ahead and removed this restriction.  To be fair though, I don't think I'd recommend setting `order` this high.  I believe every fit I looked at actually landed on a better solution when order=7 than when order=9.  So either there are numerical difficulties with high-order non-centered, non-Chebyshev/Legendre polynomials (definitely the case in the inverse case, where it's possible to end up with situations like dx~1000 pixels raised to the 9th power say) or maybe there are just more local minima to get trapped in.  I guess we could gradually increase the order when fitting to at least obtain monotonically improving results, but I haven't bothered here since the smallish field-of-view examples all do well with order=3, including LSST sims.

> (So at least the Workflow Server must know "page 3 in step 2 depends on page 3 in step 1 only".) Either way, single-page failure would not mean rollback or influence other pages, only require skip or fallback. (So the planned single-page error handling will have to be lifted up outside of the processor's loop.)

That's how I understand it as well. This also how all previous pseudo page-wise implementations in Nextflow were done (with splitting and merging the mets files). There is no dependency between the pages. Just a dependency between the steps of each page. A failed page just breaks its follow-up steps. 
Note that in case multiple pages (pages range) is passed to a single step (within a Nextflow process block), then a single broken page will also break the process block of that step and the follow up steps of all pages in the range.

> We don't have a spec on processor return codes though.

We don't really need a spec on processor return codes. The processing worker may assign the return codes based on the caught exception message from the failed processor (although I don't like that approach).

> So on the queue, it would suffice to differentiate between job status transient failure and persistent failure, and hop count in the requeued message (or the job object).

Maybe splitting the FAILURE status to two, say: "TRANSIENT FAILURE" and "PERSISTENT FAILURE" as a job status may be enough then? 

> BTW, also confer Nextflow's own error handling via [errorStrategy](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#errorstrategy) and [maxErrors](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#maxerrors)...

I am aware of these process directives. However, these are not really usable in the context of processing workers and OCR-D processors. The `errorStrategy` and `maxErrors` will apply to the process blocks which just send processing messages to the processing server and reading the results from the result queue or DB (the Nextflow script exampel I provided above).

These 2 directives are really useful when the process block contains ocr-d processor calls. Consider this simple example below. The `foo` process is executed in the HPC and if the task fails due to out of memory error, the next execution attempts will increase the memory allocation and try again. Also note that the task exit status codes are not standard and can change depending on the resource manager used in the HPC cluster.

```
process binarization {
  memory { 2.GB * task.attempt }
  errorStrategy { task.exitStatus in 137..140 ? 'retry' : 'terminate' }
  maxRetries 3

  script:
  """
  call the ocr-d processor here
  """
}
``` 
Of course, this may also be useful for Workflow Server level error handling.

I am considering using normal spark job instead of barrier mode job, the reason is:

1. Using normal spark job, spark tasks can automatically retry individually, we don't need to write additional retry logic, But, in barrier mode, if one spark task fails, it will cause all other spark tasks killed.

2. Using normal spark job, we can support failover when a spark worker physical machine crashes. (spark will try to re-schedule the spark task to other spark worker nodes)

3. Using barrier mode job, if the cluster resources does not satisfy "idle spark task slots >= argument `num_spark_task`", then the barrier job will stuck and wait until enough idle task slots available. But, this behavior is not user-friendly, on a shared spark cluster, user is hard to estimate how many idle tasks available at a time, But, if using normal spark job, it can launch job with less spark tasks, and when more task slots become available, it continues to launch tasks on new available slots, and user can see the ray cluster worker increases when more slots available.

"yes", we've talked a bit in the past about whether this should be the case or not. That is, if a span is active and you spawn a new execution, should every span in that new execution be parented to the creator?

Where we end up having problems is with context propagation across processes/the network. e.g. you make an http call from Service A to Service B: Service B handles the request and between finishing the request and handling the next one it does some processing, would that processing also be parented under Service A? Most likely the answer is no, but the implementation ends up being "similar" enough to what we have here that we've decided to hold on any further changes to that behavior (the behavior in this test case is how it works today, so no changes)

In my opinion, compound-data-type data should always be stored in memory as a numpy structured array. A tuple (or list of tuples) is hard to interpret without the field names. This would also help move us away from building functionality around lists and tuples. We could define `TimeSeriesReferenceVectorData.add_row` to take in a tuple and convert it to the correct structured array element (or take in a structured array object and check its fields) before appending it to the internal numpy structured array. 

And to answer your question about VectorData and compound types, see https://github.com/hdmf-dev/hdmf/issues/655. If the compound type is defined in the schema, then before write, I believe it is  retrieved as a list of tuples, and after write, it is retrieved as a 1D numpy structured array.
